data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was a consequence of a pandemic or series of virulent plagues that swept through the densely populated and interconnected palatial centers, causing acute demographic and social collapse from which the complex administrative structures could not recover.", "mechanism": "The highly concentrated urban centers and extensive trade routes of the Late Bronze Age provided an ideal environment for the rapid transmission of infectious diseases. A pathogen with high mortality, introduced via trade or migration, would have decimated the bureaucratic and artisan classes essential to the palace economy. The loss of specialized knowledge (e.g., Linear B scribes) and labor led to a rapid systems collapse, with survivors abandoning the plague-ridden centers. The 'Sea Peoples' migrations were a secondary effect of these demographic pressures and power vacuums.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological or textual evidence shows the destruction layers are associated with widespread conflagration and violent trauma on human remains, rather than mass burials without perimortem violence.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Ancient DNA analysis of remains from the period reveals no novel pathogen with a high mortality rate across the affected regions.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Egypt's survival is attributed to its more dispersed population along the Nile and a lower dependency on the kind of dense, centralized palace complexes that were epidemiological tinderboxes. The Nile's geography may have also acted as a natural quarantine barrier."}
